cue: How do you say someone refuses to see what's obvious?

es.literal: to have cobwebs in one's eyes
es.meaning: To fail to see what is plainly there.
es.example: ¿No viste el error? Tienes telarañas en los ojos.
es.exampleTranslation: You didn't spot the mistake? You must be blind.
es.regionNote: Reviewer to confirm MX currency and to check the RAE headword spelling (telaraña).

it.text: Avere le fette di salame sugli occhi
it.literal: to have slices of salami over one's eyes
it.meaning: To fail to see what is plainly there.
it.example: Non hai visto l'errore? Hai le fette di salame sugli occhi.
it.exampleTranslation: You didn't spot the mistake? You must be blind.

bridge.note: Blindness explained by what is stuck over the eyes, and the two languages reach for opposite kinds of object. Spanish grows COBWEBS — something that accumulated slowly because you were not looking, neglect made visible. Italy applies SALAMI — placed there, absurd, comic, and above all edible, because Italian reaches for the kitchen even to insult you. One blames time, the other blames lunch.

interference.es_to_it: Cobwebs mean nothing here in Italian; the eyes are covered with slices of salami — fette di salame SUGLI occhi.
interference.it_to_es: 'Tener rodajas de salami en los ojos' is not Spanish; the eyes gather cobwebs.

review.notes: ES side discovered in the Spanish idioms book Joe is reading, 2026-07-21 (discovery only; all glosses and examples authored fresh). IT counterpart from Claude's recall — UNVERIFIED. Queued from the Instagram set with the IT side only; the book supplies the ES. Neglect-vs-absurdity framing.
